observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to review and correct the recording of transferred PPE items to Inventory account to reflect their proper valuation in the financial statements and review existing procedures for asset transfers to ensure compliance with COA Circular No. 2024-006 dated March 14, 2024.
Moreover, it was agreed that all items below the capitalization threshold that were acquired during the year and have already been issued to end-users should be adjusted and recognized as expense, while those acquired and issued before CY 2024 should be recorded as prior period adjustments.
Prior to the agreed review of the valuation of inventories and taking up of the necessary adjusting journal entries, it is the management’s plan to conduct on March 24 to April initial physical count of all items below the capitalization threshold of ₱50,000.00 which will be done per department. Thereafter, those personnel who are found to be in custody of the items shall be issued with ICS.
It is emphasized that the point of this observation is not the misclassification but the understatement of the amount recognized as inventory due to the recording of the amount net of accumulated depreciation instead of the acquisition cost. The correction of the misstatement should be prioritized in order that the financial statements beginning CY 2024 be presented fairly. On the planned initial physical count, it is suggested that it should be done during the actual one-time cleansing to avoid duplicating efforts. It is further recommended reviewing the relevant circulars in order to strategize efficiently.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to provide the Municipal Accountant at the beginning of the year, a duly certified list showing the names of taxpayers and the amounts due and collectible for the year as basis in setting up the RPT/ SET receivables and deferred income as instructed by Sec. 20 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System Volume I.
We further recommended that the suggested adjusting entry be taken up to match the records of the Municipal Treasurer:
GF:
SEF:
We appreciate the Municipal Treasurer’s commitment to implement the recommended actions. But again, we stress the importance of obtaining optimal collection of real property and special education taxes of all the assessed real property units by closely coordinating with the Municipal Assessor. On the other hand, the Municipal Accountant’s lack of response on the recommended adjusting entry may compromise not only the fair presentation of the affected accounts in the CY 2024 financial statements but in the succeeding years as well.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant  draw the necessary adjusting entries for the current year as suggested and presented in the preceding table to correct the affected accounts in the year-end financial statements and arrive at a reliable balance.
As all the shares of the advance payments collected in CY 2024 were already remitted by the Municipal Treasurer to the recipient LGUs, the Municipal Accountant commits to the accurate recording of advance payments beginning CY 2025.
The audit team would like to emphasize that unless the erroneous entries are corrected, the affected accounts will not be fairly presented in the CY 2024 financial statements and in the years thereafter.

We recommended that management direct the Municipal Accountant to draw the necessary adjusting entries to correct the affected real and nominal accounts in the non-recording of these biological assets to ensure fair presentation of financial statements in accordance with paragraphs 16-17 of IPSAS No. 27 and paragraphs 96-97 of IPSAS No. 23.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to:
Prepare the following adjusting entry to reverse the non-existing payable balances in the case of those accounts with no supporting documents:
Prepare the following adjusting journal entry to recognize legitimate payables in the case of those transactions not recorded as of year-end:
The Municipal Accountant disclosed that the payable items that are outstanding for more than two (2) years will be reversed in CY 2025. To fund the payment of the payables that were unaccounted due to the incompleteness of supporting documents as of cut-off date set for withholding tax purposes, a request for the inclusion of such in a CY 2025 supplemental budget is planned to be endorsed.
Delaying the reversal of payables that fall under the provisions of Section 98 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 until the next calendar year will result in the CY 2024 financial statements remaining misstated. The same effect applies to the receipt of purchased items by the MDRRMO in CY 2024 which were not recorded in the books.
On the management’s plan to settle the received items through a supplemental budget, it can be inferred that the Municipal Accountant remains firm in not recognizing the legitimate payables as of year-end despite the recommendation as charges and penalties will surely be incurred. However, based on the audit of the approved obligation requests charged against the 5% DRRM Fund, these transactions were already obligated thus, there is no need to fund its payment from a new revenue source. Moreover, Section 350 of Republic Act No. 7160 provides that all lawful expenditures and obligations incurred during a fiscal year shall be taken up in the accounts of that year.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to consider taking up the suggested adjusting journal entry:
The Journal Entry Voucher No. 2025-03-002215 is dated March 12, 2025, thus, as of December 31, 2024, the affected accounts were still misstated.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to take up the suggested adjusting journal entry to fairly present the accounts in the financial statements:
The Journal Entry Voucher No. 2025-03-002194 is dated March 12, 2025, thus, as of December 31, 2024, the affected accounts were still misstated.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to create subsidiary ledgers for the current and non-current portion of the Loans Payable – Domestic account in order to keep track of the amounts payable within the next year  or beyond and be presented fairly in the Statement of Financial Position and Notes to the Financial Statements as mandated by par. 80(c) of IPSAS 1.
The Municipal Accountant explained that amortization schedule from the Land Bank of the Philippines will be secured to obtain accurate amount of yearly principal to be paid. Immediately after obtaining the schedule, subsidiary ledgers will be created to monitor the current and non-current portion of the Loans Payable – Domestic account.
During the exit conference and even from previous online communication with the Municipal Accountant, the Municipality was able to secure the amortization schedule from the bank and identified that ₱6,053,025.70 is to be paid in CY 2025. By then, subsidiary ledgers would have already been created as per commitment but as of this writing, the audit team has not received an amended Statement of Financial Position as of December 31, 2024.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to use the appropriate accounts in recording all cash advances in accordance with the prescribed chart of accounts in COA Circular No. 2015-009.  Further, we recommended and  the DO - Designate agreed to use a separate cashbook for special purpose/time-bound undertakings and record transactions based on the nature of the cash advances drawn.

We recommended and the management agreed to secure first an authorization from the Local Sanggunian prior to implementation of PPAs funded out of the lumpsum/ generic appropriation of the 20% Local Development Fund.
Through Supplemental Budget No. 6 CY 2024, ₱1.975 million of the ₱2 million  appropriated for the sustenance of Solid Waste Management Program was approved to be realigned as additional fund for the purchase of garbage truck thus, there was authority from the Local Sanggunian.
For the Agricultural, Coastal Resource Management and Waterworks Development Programs, work and financial plans incorporated in the Project Procurement Management Plan (PPMP) will be presented for approval in the next calendar years.
On the additional amount for the renovation of Duero Cultural Center, no further authority was requested as it was initially thought to be specific given that the LGU has only one cultural center. In future cases like this, it will be made sure that the Local Sanggunian’s approval be sought.
We would like to emphasize that when projects are initially described in vague or general terms, obtaining subsequent approvals becomes a lengthy and time-consuming process. In order not to incur further delays, we believe that it is most effective for the planned programs, projects and activities integrated in the annual budget to be clearly specified. We are pleased that the management concurs with this approach through the planned incorporation in the next calendar years’ PPMP.

We recommended and the management agreed to require all employees authorized to render overtime to support their DTRs with a record of their time-in and time-out for all work hours rendered, using biometrics or logbooks duly verified by their respective supervisors. We also recommended and the management agreed to require the concerned Job Order personnel to prepare and submit Quantified Overtime Accomplishment Reports duly signed by the employee and supervisor pursuant to Section 5.10 of COA Circular 2012-001 to ensure that the services were actually rendered in accordance with the assigned tasks.

We recommended and the management agreed to stop charging expenses against the 5% DRRM Fund that is not within the projects and activities for disaster prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response and rehabilitation and recovery enumerated in NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the MDRRM Officer to strive for the timely and full implementation of LDRRM programs, projects and activities to achieve the goals, objectives and desired benefits of the fund.
As explained by the MDRRM Officer, the programs to conduct quarterly emergency drill, DRR/CCA community caravans, Oplan Ligtas na Pamayanan community caravans, IECs to the residents of 21 brgys., IECs to End Local Communist Armed Conflicts and apprehend illegal quarrying operations were actually conducted but no funds were used. The fund used for quarterly preparedness meetings is no longer the 5% DRRM Fund but the General Fund. The appropriation for the maintenance and re-installation of Public Address System was put on hold and will only be utilized once the construction of the new command center is completed. For the regular monitoring and maintenance of evacuation center, such was not utilized because the facility is not yet functional. The Ladderized Incident Command System (ICS) Training and other trainings appropriation was also not utilized for the reason that the training, which is facilitated only by the Office of Civil Defense Regional Office No. VII, has no slots available.
The unutilized Trust Liabilities – DRRMF was appropriated for the purchase of rescue vehicle but not yet utilized for it is not enough. The additional appropriation from the CY 2025 DRRMF would complete the funds to materialize the purchase of the vehicle.
In the next years, the Municipality commits to strive more and closely monitor the charging of funds. Also, the creation of MDRRM staff is anticipated so that the LDRMM programs, projects and activities will likely be achieved.
The audit team reminds the management on its direct knowledge on the on-going and future ventures of the Municipality. This knowledge must serve as the basis on the future action of the management. If a certain project hinders the implementation of its future sub-projects, such sub-projects should not be planned in the first place but instead to appropriate the fund for a project that needs immediate implementation. In cases where due to unexpected occurrences, project implementation is no longer possible, the management is encouraged to use the appropriation for another project that is aligned with the Municipality’s purpose. It is recognized that the management commits to strive for the implementation and monitoring of the 5% DRRM Fund in order to provide its constituents a disaster-resilient community.

We recommended and the management agreed to review the HEA granted to the qualified recipients and rectify errors causing over or underpayment to promote fair compensation to the BHWs based on their actual services rendered.
The management with the support of the Rural Health Unit will endeavor to collect from the overpaid BHWs and compensate those underpaid personnel.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer agreed to coordinate so that taxes withheld in the SEF and TF be transferred to the GF first before payment/remittance to the BIR is made.

We recommended and the management agreed that all collections be remitted intact and daily to avoid misuse of government funds.  Likewise, management agreed to call the attention of the above collectors to remind them of their duties and responsibilities; otherwise, they should stop collecting various income payments to ensure safety and proper accountability of government funds.

We recommended that Management require the Bids and Awards Committee to revisit and review the procurement procedures prescribed in the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA No. 9184, particularly on post-qualification process under Section 34.3(b)(iii) and develop a standardized checklist for post-qualification assessment to ensure uniform and thorough evaluation of suppliers.
The management explained that the supplier was given award for the procurement of ICT equipment due to their business permit, which includes the line of business "computers, parts, and accessories”. After a thorough eligibility checking, it was found out that the supplier fully meets all of the eligibility requirements. Their inclusion of this specific line of business on their business permit indicates their capability to provide the necessary ICT equipment. This business permit demonstrated that supplier is engaged in the sale and distribution of products directly related to the procurement needs, which made them a relevant supplier for the equipment required.

We recommended and the GSO – Designate agreed to conduct an inventory of the Christmas Decorations procured in 2022 and 2024 showing in detail their current state and submit the report to the Audit Team for evaluation.
We further recommended and the management agreed to consider economical ways to rehabilitate and make the decorations useful again.
Management explained that for the Beautification of Local Tourism Park and Plaza project in CY 2022, the 4 pieces of GI pipes were used as poles for the 30 feet Christmas Tree of Light. These were altered and cut into two pieces to fit beside the cultural center/gymnasium. Currently, the LED lights and plastic vinyl comprising the said tree, the four (4) feet tall "I love Duero" letterings and Capiz tree balls are already worn out and cannot be reused but are still kept in the storage area. The stars are also beyond repair. The three (3) feet tall "Merry Christmas and Happy New Year” letterings were repaired and used in CY 2024. Some decorative roses with lights were also used in CY 2024.
For the same project in CY 2024, the ten-year life span applies only to the four (4) steel poles used as main frames of the Christmas Tree. All other fabrication were reckoned to be good as one-time use like the steel bars for the 1 set reindeer with sleigh, 1 set of lighted balls,  30 sets of cone-shaped decorative tree, 20 sets of palm tree lightings, 40 sets of star-shaped lantern, 20 sets of lighted arches, 15 sets of Capiz balls, and 15 sets of fine tree stars. Most of the items in small sizes are stored in the storage area. However, the bigger sizes are still installed but are slightly damaged due to the variation in temperature. The mentioned items, if repaired, can be utilized in the next Christmas season.
With the noted observation, the Municipality shall consider economical ways and be observant on this concern moving forward.

observations.recommendation: We recommend the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant adhere strictly to the provisions of Section 271(d) of Republic Act 7160 to avoid the unnecessary accumulation of trust funds in the municipal treasury and hamper the implementation of the barangay projects programmed during the year.
observations.managementAction: Management will comply.
observations.reason: Account for Repair and Maintenance expenses of school buildings were not booked as asset of the LGU. The eNGAS requires the asset account if the repair and maintenance account is used. Cut-off period for revenue collectors to remit/submit reports of their daily collections was not observed at all times.

observations.recommendation: We recommend the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to charge expenses to its appropriate expense account within the approved budget to avoid the unnecessary accumulation of trust funds in the municipal treasury and hamper the implementation of the barangay projects programmed during the year.
observations.managementAction: Enforced daily remittance of collections by the revenue clerks.

observations.recommendation: Treasurer should facilitate the updating and reconciling of records, thus presenting fairly the actual liability of the local government unit and actual income of the barangay and the release of the current year’s real property taxes to the barangay immediately, as well as the succeeding years’ share on said income within five(5) days after the end of each quarter to augment its meager resources and ensure its prompt utilization.
observations.managementAction: Still to appoint MDRRM officer and personnel

observations.recommendation: We recommend that management instruct the Municipal Treasurer to enforce the daily/regular remittance/turn over of collections by the revenue collectors.
observations.managementAction: Still to appoint MDRRM officer and personnel

observations.recommendation: We recommend that management appoint officials and personnel to conduct DRRM activities to assure that risk of lives and property be reduced in times of calamity and necessary and adequate disaster preparedness could be undertaken. Further, we also recommend that MDRRMO be strengthened for them to be responsive in times of calamities.
observations.managementAction: Management will comply.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that management stop the practice of acquiring supplies and materials  on a staggered basis and adhere to the provisions of Section 10 of Article IV of Republic Act 9184, which requires that all procurement shall be done through competitive public bidding to ensure that procurement of goods and services are obtained at prices which are most advantageous to the municipality. Likewise, strictly comply with the conditions enumerated under Section 52 and 53, rules XVI of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 in the event that the original mode of procurement as indicated in the APP cannot be pushed through. Limit the use of alternative mode of procurement only when circumstances warrant and fall within the provisions of Section 52 of Rule XVI of RA 9184. Require the accounting/treasury sections to verify the origin of these accounts and when pertinent documents are available send proper bills to debtors for its collection. Pursue continued collection, otherwise, request  for write-off of the accounts from the Central Office of the Commission on Audit when collectibility is nil, supported with the required documents.
observations.managementAction: Require the accounting/treasury sections to verify the origin of these accounts and when pertinent documents are available send proper bills to debtors for its collection. Pursue continued collection, otherwise, request  for write-off of the accounts from the Central Office of the Commission on Audit when collectibility is nil, supported with the required documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that Municipal Officials limit the utilization of the 20% Development Fund to viable and sustainable programs, projects and activities that directly generate jobs and livelihood opportunities to its constituents in accordance with Department of Interior and Local Government Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1.

We recommend that Management require the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and the Municipal Engineer to closely coordinate on the immediate implementation of the priority development projects, programs and activities funded under the 20% Development Fund during the year the funds are actually appropriated in order to ensure that the intended beneficiaries could avail of the maximum benefits and the desired outcomes from the said programs or projects.

We recommend that management require the inventory committee/team to prioritize the conduct of a complete inventory count of all properties and submit the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment to the auditor in accordance with Section 124 of Manual on New Government Accounting System (NGAS), Volume I. Henceforth, require the reconciliation of records maintained by the Office of the Municipal Treasurer and effect the necessary adjustments when appropriate.

We recommend that management require the Municipal Treasurer to prepare certified list of taxpayers and amount due and collectible and submit this to the Municipal Accountant as a basis in taking up the receivables and deferred income in order for the financial statements to be fairly presented.

We recommend that the concerned officers should see to it that all request for the procurement of medicines by the LGU should be supported with a certification by the Municipal Health Officer that the drugs being requisitioned or procured fall within and conform with PNDF, Volume I, current edition, pursuant to the above mentioned EO and AO.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant guided by COA Circular 2016-005, should initiate the verification, validation, review, analysis and reconciliation of the abovementioned dormant accounts to effect necessary adjusting/ correcting journal entries for the eventual disposition thereof. Further, the said accounts can be requested for write-off as these affect the fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommend to the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to submit report on Sources and Utilization of Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund (DRRMF) on or before the 15th day after the end of each month. Also, we would like to highlight to the management to be continually guided by the provisions and laws regarding the allocation and utilization of LDRRMF.
We recommend the management to limit the utilization of the 70% of the 5% LDRRMF to programs, activities and projects that are related to disaster prevention, mitigation and preparedness.

We recommend to the management to instruct the inventory committee to conduct physical inventory on the remaining Agricultural Produce for Distribution and require the maintenance of Ledger Cards and submission of monthly inventory report to the Municipal Accountant.

We recommend the management to relieve the disbursing officer from her collecting function to prevent the possibility of committing errors and irregularities in any transactions. Disbursing and collecting function are separate and distinct and should be performed by one person. An adequate internal control system is essential in the local government unit and absence of the latter leads to concealment of errors which may possibly result to irregularities.
